subject: ap-psychology
theme: Clinical Psychology: Understanding and Treating Psychological Disorders

core_concepts.title: Defining and Classifying Psychological Disorders
core_concepts.summary: The DSM-5 (Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders, 5th edition) is the primary diagnostic reference in the U.S., defining disorders by symptom criteria that cause significant distress or functional impairment. The biopsychosocial model is the dominant framework — disorders result from interactions among biological factors (genetics, neurotransmitters), psychological factors (cognitive patterns, trauma history), and social factors (relationships, cultural context). The diathesis-stress model proposes that disorders emerge when a pre-existing vulnerability (diathesis) interacts with environmental stressors. The medical model treats disorders like diseases with biological causes, while critics note that diagnostic labels can stigmatize individuals and that symptoms vary across cultures.

core_concepts.title: Anxiety, OCD, and Trauma-Related Disorders
core_concepts.summary: Anxiety disorders share excessive fear and avoidance. Generalized anxiety disorder (GAD): diffuse, uncontrollable worry across many topics for 6+ months. Panic disorder: recurrent unexpected panic attacks with anticipatory anxiety. Specific phobia: marked, persistent fear of a specific object or situation. Social anxiety disorder: fear of social scrutiny. PTSD (post-traumatic stress disorder): following exposure to trauma — re-experiencing (flashbacks, nightmares), avoidance, negative cognitions/mood, and hyperarousal lasting 1+ months. OCD (obsessive-compulsive disorder): intrusive obsessions driving repetitive compulsions — separated from anxiety disorders in DSM-5. Classical conditioning explains phobias; exposure-based treatments use extinction. The amygdala plays a key role in fear processing.

core_concepts.title: Mood Disorders and Schizophrenia
core_concepts.summary: Major depressive disorder (MDD): 2+ weeks of depressed mood or anhedonia plus 4+ additional symptoms. Bipolar disorder: cycling between depressive episodes and manic episodes (or hypomania in Bipolar II). Manic episodes: elevated/irritable mood, decreased sleep, racing thoughts, grandiosity, impulsivity — lasting 7+ days and causing marked impairment. Schizophrenia: positive symptoms (hallucinations — most often auditory; delusions — fixed false beliefs; disorganized speech/behavior) and negative symptoms (flat affect, alogia, avolition). The dopamine hypothesis: excess dopaminergic activity contributes to positive symptoms — supported by antipsychotics that block D2 receptors. Schizophrenia heritability ~80% but environment matters (identical twin concordance ~50%). Diathesis-stress applies to all mood disorders and schizophrenia.

core_concepts.title: Personality Disorders
core_concepts.summary: Personality disorders involve inflexible, maladaptive personality patterns causing long-term distress or impairment — must be stable across situations and dating to adolescence/early adulthood. Antisocial personality disorder: pervasive disregard for others' rights, lack of empathy, deceitfulness, impulsivity, no remorse — preceded in childhood by conduct disorder. Borderline personality disorder (BPD): unstable identity, fear of abandonment, intense/unstable relationships, emotional dysregulation, self-harm — treated with dialectical behavior therapy (DBT). Narcissistic personality disorder: grandiosity, entitlement, need for admiration, lack of empathy. Personality disorders are ego-syntonic (the person does not usually see the pattern as problematic) — making treatment difficult.

core_concepts.title: Psychological Therapies and Biomedical Treatments
core_concepts.summary: Psychoanalysis uses free association, dream interpretation, and transference analysis to uncover unconscious conflicts. Behavioral therapies apply learning principles — systematic desensitization (classical conditioning: gradual exposure + relaxation for phobias), flooding (immediate maximal exposure), and token economies (operant conditioning: secondary reinforcers for desired behaviors). Cognitive therapy (Beck) identifies and restructures cognitive distortions (all-or-nothing thinking, catastrophizing, overgeneralization). REBT (Ellis) uses the A-B-C model to vigorously dispute irrational beliefs. CBT combines cognitive and behavioral approaches — most evidence-based treatment for anxiety, depression, OCD. Person-centered therapy (Rogers): unconditional positive regard, empathy, and genuineness. Biomedical: SSRIs for depression/anxiety (increase serotonin), antipsychotics for schizophrenia (block dopamine), lithium for bipolar. ECT is effective for severe treatment-resistant depression. The therapeutic alliance is the strongest predictor of therapy outcomes across all modalities.

key_terms.definition: Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders, 5th edition — the primary U.S. diagnostic reference
Framework attributing psychological disorders to interacting biological, psychological, and social factors
Disorders emerge when a predisposing vulnerability (diathesis) is triggered by environmental stress
Excessive, uncontrollable worry across many areas of life, lasting 6+ months
Recurrent unexpected panic attacks plus anticipatory anxiety about future attacks
Marked, excessive fear of a specific object or situation leading to avoidance
Trauma-related disorder: re-experiencing, avoidance, negative cognitions, hyperarousal for 1+ months after trauma
Obsessive-compulsive disorder: intrusive obsessions driving repetitive compulsions
2+ weeks of depressed mood or anhedonia plus 4+ symptoms (sleep, appetite, energy, concentration, worthlessness, suicidal ideation)
Cycling between depressive episodes and manic or hypomanic episodes
Elevated/irritable mood, decreased sleep need, grandiosity, racing thoughts, impulsivity for 7+ days with marked impairment
Psychotic disorder with positive symptoms (hallucinations, delusions) and negative symptoms (flat affect, avolition)
Sensory perception without external stimulus — most commonly auditory voices in schizophrenia
Fixed false belief held despite clear contradicting evidence (e.g., persecutory, grandiose)
Schizophrenia symptoms representing excess of normal function — hallucinations, delusions, disorganized speech
Schizophrenia symptoms representing diminishment of normal function — flat affect, alogia, avolition
Excess dopamine activity (especially mesolimbic) contributes to positive symptoms of schizophrenia
Persistent disregard for others' rights, deceitfulness, lack of remorse — must have conduct disorder before age 15
Unstable identity, fear of abandonment, emotional dysregulation, self-harm — treated with DBT
Behavioral phobia treatment: gradual exposure to feared stimulus paired with relaxation
OCD treatment: exposure to obsession triggers while preventing compulsive responses
Beck's term for irrational thinking patterns — all-or-nothing thinking, catastrophizing, overgeneralization
Ellis's rational emotive behavior therapy — A-B-C model to vigorously dispute irrational beliefs
Cognitive-behavioral therapy — combines thought-challenging (cognitive) with behavior change (behavioral) techniques
Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors — antidepressants that increase synaptic serotonin
Medications that block dopamine D2 receptors — primarily treat positive symptoms of schizophrenia
Mood stabilizer used to treat bipolar disorder
Electroconvulsive therapy — controlled electrical brain stimulation for severe treatment-resistant depression
The quality of the relationship between therapist and client — the strongest predictor of therapy outcomes
Behavioral technique using secondary reinforcers (tokens) exchangeable for rewards to shape desired behaviors

exam_tip: For the AP exam, know the symptom criteria for the most commonly tested disorders: MDD (2 weeks, depressed mood or anhedonia + 4 symptoms), bipolar (mania + depression cycling), schizophrenia (positive vs. negative symptoms), GAD (chronic worry 6+ months), panic disorder (unexpected attacks), PTSD (trauma + 4 symptom clusters). For treatments, match the technique to its theoretical basis: systematic desensitization (classical conditioning), token economy (operant conditioning), CBT (cognitive + behavioral), free association and dream interpretation (psychoanalytic). The therapeutic alliance being the strongest outcome predictor appears frequently as a trap answer in treatment-effectiveness questions.
